g.data-narration: E-Waste Management Rules 2011 - An important topic for sustainable technology and environmental protection.
g.text: Enacted under Environment Protection Act, 1986 | Effective from May 1, 2012

g.data-narration: E-waste includes discarded electrical devices like computers, phones, and TVs that have reached end-of-life.
g.text: Electronic waste refers to discarded electrical or electronic devices that have reached
their end-of-life. Includes: computers, laptops, mobile phones, televisions,
refrigerators, printers, keyboards, and IT equipment.

g.data-narration: India generates e-waste at 30% annual rate, making proper management crucial for environmental protection.
g.text: • E-waste is growing at 30% per year in India - one of the fastest waste streams
• Contains hazardous substances: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Chromium
• IT industry is a major contributor - CSE engineers must understand this!

g.data-narration: The Rules cover all electrical equipment dependent on electric currents or electromagnetic fields to function.
g.text: "Electrical and Electronic Equipment" (EEE) - equipment that depends on
electric currents or electromagnetic fields to function properly.
Scope: Computers, Laptops, Mobile Phones, TVs, AC, Washing Machines,
Printers, Servers, Networking Equipment, IT/Telecom devices.

g.data-narration: E-waste sources are categorized as household and institutional, with IT sector being a major contributor.
E-waste contains toxic substances like lead, mercury, cadmium, and brominated flame retardants that harm health.
Producers must collect e-waste, set up take-back systems, achieve 30% collection targets, and maintain records.
g.text: 1. Set up collection centers or take-back systems for end-of-life products
2. Achieve collection target: 30% of weight of EEE sold (initial target)
3. Channelize e-waste to authorized recyclers only
4. Maintain records of e-waste collected and channelized

g.data-narration: Consumers must use authorized collection centers, never dispose e-waste with municipal waste.
Authorized recyclers must obtain SPCB authorization and follow strict environmental standards for processing.
g.text: • Obtain authorization from State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)
• Follow specific procedures for recycling that prevent environmental pollution
• Install air pollution control equipment and wastewater treatment facilities
• Maintain safe handling procedures for hazardous materials

g.data-narration: Extended Producer Responsibility makes manufacturers financially responsible for end-of-life management of their products.
g.text: EPR is a key principle where producers are financially and operationally responsible for
the end-of-life management of their products. This incentivizes designing products
that are easier to recycle and contain fewer hazardous materials.

g.data-narration: The rules establish a formal collection mechanism with authorized centers, manifests for tracking, and proper channelization.
g.text: Manifest system ensures traceability from collection to final disposal

g.data-narration: Open burning and disposal in municipal bins are strictly prohibited due to toxic fume and leaching risks.
Example: A manufacturer selling 10,000 computers (10kg each) must collect 30,000 kg of e-waste.
g.text: Problem: Manufacturer sold 10,000 desktop computers (10 kg each) in 2012
Target: 30% of weight of EEE placed in market

g.data-narration: Key points: Rules effective from May 2012, four stakeholders with responsibilities, 30% collection target, EPR principle.
g.text: ✓ Effective Date: May 1, 2012 (under Environment Protection Act, 1986)
✓ Four Stakeholders: Producers, Consumers, Dealers, Authorized Recyclers
✓ Collection Target: Initial 30% of weight of EEE sold
✓ EPR: Producers financially responsible for end-of-life management
✓ Prohibitions: Open burning, municipal disposal, landfill dumping banned

g.data-narration: Remember: parent Act (EPA 1986), EPR principle, five hazardous substances, and differences from 2016 rules.
g.text: 2. Remember: 5 hazardous substances (Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Cr6+, BFRs)
3. Know: EPR principle thoroughly - frequently asked in exams
4. Note: Rules 2011 replaced by 2016 rules with stricter provisions

g.data-narration: This completes the visual lesson on E-Waste Management Rules 2011.
